question: A 45-year-old man with Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis is found to have a 3 cm mass in the right lobe of the liver. Staging workup confirms the tumor has no vascular invasion or distant metastasis. What is the best treatment option for this patient?

options.B:
| Hepatic Oncology | Hepatocellular Carcinoma 
Question 54
A 45-year-old man with Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis is found to have a 3 cm mass in the right lobe of the liver. Staging workup confirms the tumor has no vascular invasion or distant metastasis. What is the best treatment option for this patient?

markdown_debrief:
### SHARP 3.0 Cognitive & Surgical Debrief

* **S – Set the Stage (The Verdict & Diagnostic Pivot)**
 Verdict: Correct: C. Liver transplantation.
 The Pivot: For a patient with early-stage hepatocellular carcinoma (single tumor $\le 5\text{ cm}$ matching Milan Criteria) who has decompensated Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis, a liver transplant is the treatment of choice. It cures the underlying liver failure while removing the malignancy.
* **H – Highlight Excellence (The Surgical "Why" & Pathology)**
  * **Surgical Mechanism:** In patients with Child-Pugh Class C liver disease, the liver has minimal functional reserve, presenting with poor protein synthesis, ascites, and encephalopathy. Performing a standard hepatic resection will cause acute postoperative liver failure and carries an unacceptably high mortality rate. A liver transplant addresses both issues: it removes the tumor with clear margins and replaces the failing cirrhotic liver with a healthy graft, resolving the portal hypertension and liver failure.
 Clinical Execution: The patient's tumor fits within the Milan Criteria (one lesion $\le 5\text{ cm}$, no vascular invasion, no extrahepatic spread). This makes him an ideal candidate for liver transplantation, which offers a 5-year survival rate exceeding 70%. While on the transplant waiting list, short-term bridging therapies like radiofrequency ablation can be used to limit tumor growth.
* **A – Address the Gaps (Systematic Distractor Demolition)**
  * **Distractor Breakdown:**
 Option A (Incorrect): Surgical resection is contraindicated in Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis due to a high risk of liver failure and death. Resection is reserved for Child-Pugh Class A patients with normal portal pressures.
 Option B (Incorrect): RFA can destroy local tissue, but it is used as a bridging therapy or for patients who cannot undergo surgery; it does not treat the underlying liver failure.
 Option D (Incorrect): Sorafenib is a systemic tyrosine kinase inhibitor used for advanced, unresectable hepatocellular carcinoma (Stage C or D with vascular invasion or metastases), rather than localized, transplant-eligible disease.
 Option E (Incorrect): Palliative care alone is premature because the patient meets the criteria for a liver transplant, which offers a chance for a definitive cure.
* **R – Review Learning Points (Evidence-Based Framework)**
  * **Conceptual Overview:** Selecting a treatment for hepatocellular carcinoma requires evaluating both the extent of the tumor and the severity of the patient's underlying liver disease using the Barcelona Clinic Liver Cancer (BCLC) system.
  * **Classification / Management Framework:**
Liver Function Status
Tumor Burden / Criteria
BCLC Stage
Preferred Surgical / Clinical Strategy
Child-Pugh A; normal portal pressure
Single lesion, any location
Stage 0 / A
Hepatic Resection (Segmentectomy) 

Child-Pugh B / C

Single $\le 5$ cm (Milan Criteria)
Stage A

Liver Transplantation
Child-Pugh A / B
Multiple tumors or $>5\text{ cm}$
Stage B
Transarterial Chemoembolization (TACE)
Child-Pugh A / B
Portal vein invasion / Metastasis
Stage C
Systemic Therapy (Atezolizumab + Bevacizumab / Sorafenib) 
* **P – Plan for Improvement (The Exam Heuristic)**
 The Board Pearl: If a patient has hepatocellular carcinoma that fits within the Milan criteria ($\le 5\text{ cm}$single lesion) but has severe liver failure (Child-Pugh C), choose liver transplantation as the treatment of choice. Do not select surgical resection, as it is contraindicated in decompensated cirrhosis.

---

### Supplementary Callouts
> **Society Guideline**
> AASLD and EASL guidelines recommend liver transplantation as the gold standard for patients with hepatocellular carcinoma who meet the Milan criteria and have decompensated liver function (Child-Pugh B or C).
> **Key Takeaway**
> Liver transplantation is the preferred treatment for early-stage HCC in patients with Child-Pugh Class C cirrhosis, as it addresses both the tumor and the liver failure.
